main_summary.summary: Purcell Maurice Conway, born 1948 in St. Augustine, Florida, recounts his entry into civil rights activism as a teenager in the Lincolnville Black community during the 1963-1964 St. Augustine campaign. Conway describes being raised by his great-great-grandmother in Lincolnville, his early work selling papers at the Castillo de San Marcos, and his radicalization through encounters with segregation at the St. Augustine Record office and during a job his mother arranged with a white family. He recounts hearing Florida Memorial College students discuss nonviolent demonstration tactics, his participation in marches under Dr. Robert B. Hayling's leadership, and his witness of beatings at two wade-ins at St. Augustine Beach -- the first ending with him struck in the face by a white teenager and a fellow demonstrator beaten unconscious, the second ending when Florida state troopers ordered by Governor Farris Bryant beat Klan sympathizers out of the water. After the movement, Conway attended a Daytona Beach trade school, married, moved to New York, and served in the NYPD where he was a lead plaintiff in promotional-exam discrimination lawsuits that produced over 300 minority sergeants. He returned to Palm Coast, Florida and continues to advocate for the Galimore Center pool's restoration.
key_themes: Wade-in protests at St. Augustine Beach
main_summary.historical_significance:
Conway is a first-person witness to the St. Augustine wade-ins at the height of the 1964 campaign that produced the iconic images of Klan-versus-state-trooper violence on the beach -- a turning point that helped force passage of the Civil Rights Act. His account specifically documents the second wade-in where Florida state troopers, sent by Governor Farris Bryant, fought hostile whites out of the water before letting demonstrators enter; this is rare first-hand testimony of the federal-and-state pressure that broke local Klan dominance.

Conway's interview also documents the through-line from St. Augustine youth-marcher experience to lifelong civil rights litigation: his NYPD promotional-exam lawsuits produced precedent used by police departments across the United States. The interview supplements the work of Dr. Robert B. Hayling, who joins partway through, and adds a youth-marcher perspective to the better-documented adult-organizer record of the St. Augustine movement.

chapters.title: A Heavenly Place to Grow Up
chapters.summary: After Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the interview in St. Augustine for the Smithsonian and the Library of Congress, Conway recalls growing up in Lincolnville, a self-sufficient Black community where all the adults watched the children, being raised from age five by his great-great-grandmother, a Seminole and Spanish woman, and spending his days at the little links park that today houses the Galimore Center, the only place Black residents could go for recreation.

chapters.title: A Paper Boy at the Castillo
chapters.summary: At ten Conway crosses into the white area to win a paper route from the St. Augustine Record, makes only ten cents selling within Lincolnville, then moves downtown to the Castillo de San Marcos where he sells out repeatedly until two white paper boys follow him, take positions beside him, and tourists begin buying from them instead.

chapters.title: You Don't Want to Get Involved
chapters.summary: Disenchanted in high school, Conway challenges teachers over outdated textbooks and reads the books his uncle Edward Norton left behind, notices his mother forced to call white clerks miss and mister while being called only Julia, and enrolls in a work-credit program called the DCT where a grocery owner he had done accounting for refuses to sign his form until Conway threatens to quit.

chapters.title: The Only Pool in Town
chapters.summary: Conway recalls friends who drowned for lack of a place to swim and the pool Florida Memorial College opened to Black kids in summer, where, born in 1948 and now about fourteen or fifteen, he first hears young male college students talk about demonstrating and civil rights, and learns their training in nonviolence as a tactic.

chapters.title: They Were Like in a Trance
chapters.summary: Drawn more to direct speakers like Stokely Carmichael and Malcolm X but persuaded by the college students that nonviolent demonstration is the way, Conway tells his reluctant mother he will start demonstrating, and struggles to understand why the adults seem in a trance, accepting their treatment as more slave labor than labor.

chapters.title: A Sandwich by the Dog's Tray
chapters.summary: Hired to cut the grass of a white woman his mother worked for, Conway is offered a cool drink and sandwich only to find it set down beside the dog's food tray, and when his mother hears what happened on the phone she comes immediately, and from that day begins getting involved in the movement herself.

chapters.title: Don't Get Arrested
chapters.summary: Conway promises his great-great-grandmother he will not get arrested so someone can tend her, stays at the rear of marches and is often chased but never caught by police, and recalls the night his mother, father, and three brothers were all arrested while he alone stayed free, in a town where arrest for the movement became a badge of honor.

chapters.title: A Flower on the Wall
chapters.summary: Conway describes feeling like a flower on the wall as figures like Dr. Robert Hayling, Dr. King, Ralph Abernathy, and Hosea Williams began coming around, realizing only gradually that a movement had started, and recalls being pressed to help recruit people to test the newly enacted law.

chapters.title: Stand Your Ground
chapters.summary: On a demonstration at St. Augustine Beach with four other teens and a tall white man, Conway is in the surf when a crowd of whites converges from north and south, a frightened Black girl locks the group out of the car, and the mob surrounds them with weapons; a white youth punches Conway, the man with them is struck over the head with a pipe and collapses bloodied, and the mob disperses only when a lone police car approaches.

chapters.title: Beaten Into Submission
chapters.summary: After Hosea Williams vows to return stronger, a second demonstration two weeks later brings a large group back to St. Augustine Beach between a cordon of helmeted police, where Florida state troopers wade into the surf and beat the Klan and their sympathizers into submission, dragging them to the shoreline in a melee Conway had never seen the like of.

chapters.title: The Water Was Too Bloody
chapters.summary: Told by the troopers' commander that the water is safe though he cannot guarantee its quality, Conway finds the surf so thick with blood he will not go in, and is left confused that white men would fight other white men for his civil rights, an action he attributes to orders from Governor Farris Bryant and pressure from the Justice Department.

chapters.title: A Knife on a Bicycle
chapters.summary: Conway recalls that police were not sympathetic to the demonstrators and his fear of being beaten if arrested, then tells how a young white tutor took a small group of Black children to a downtown movie theater against his caution, and within minutes the children come running and screaming with a white man on a bicycle chasing them with a knife.

chapters.title: If He Moves, Kill Him
chapters.topic: The Police Dog and the Church Yard
chapters.summary: Intervening, Conway is surrounded by two policemen with a dog until his friends push the officers and run, drawing them off, and a cop orders the German Shepherd to kill him if he moves before Conway turns slowly and outruns the dog to safety on Washington Street, then hides two days and nights in a church yard while neighbors bring him water and sandwiches.

chapters.title: They Picketed Their Own Car
chapters.summary: Conway describes how white college tutors, including Gail Blattenberg of Princeton and students from Massachusetts, changed his belief that all whites hated Black people, and how when they invited Black kids to their northern homes his reluctant mother finally relented after the students made picket signs and picketed in their car to win her over.

chapters.title: This Is the Law Now
chapters.summary: The two-week trip north changes Conway's whole perspective on white people, and back home over the year or so after the Civil Rights Act he watches the white business community come around to obeying the law, returning to the same movie theater to watch a James Bond film and beginning to eat at the restaurants without trouble.

chapters.title: Two White Men Get the Certificates
chapters.summary: Conway enters a junior college trade school in Daytona Beach studying brick masonry, where he meets his future wife, and after a year and a half two white students arrive, are allowed within months to test and become masons, and when his instructor protests he is fired and the Black students are offered only a carpentry path, so they all refuse and receive nothing.

chapters.title: Worse in the North
chapters.summary: Married to a woman from New York, Conway moves north and finds racism worse than the South, overt below the Mason-Dixon line but a pat on the back and a smile while they stab you in the north, and works five years in the post office doing a supervisor's accounting for less pay before passing the police department's test.

chapters.title: You Curse Me, I Curse Back
chapters.summary: In the New York City police department Conway finds rampant, rank-based racism but refuses the disrespect as he had as a teen, demanding to be treated as a human being regardless of rank, joined by other officers who came out of the civil rights movement, and sues the department and city over discriminatory promotional exams, winning.

chapters.title: Quota Sergeants
chapters.summary: Conway recounts winning the lawsuit whose criteria were used nationally and the sergeants test that produced over three hundred minority candidates derided as quota sergeants, and how, retiring as a lieutenant, he trained hundreds of officers, mostly white, charging them to treat every woman in a Black neighborhood like their own mother or answer to him.

chapters.title: Drug-Ravaged and Red-Lined
chapters.summary: Bringing his children down every summer over the years, Conway grows disheartened at the non-improvement of Black life in St. Augustine, returning to abandoned houses, drug-ravaged areas, and Black seniors unable to keep their properties, recognizing the red-lining he had witnessed in New York playing out in the gentrification of Lincolnville.

chapters.title: The Buddy System
chapters.summary: Asked how his St. Augustine experience shaped his later life, Conway, raised to be neither violent nor passive, describes challenging the buddy system in the police department as a quiet form of racism, since the qualified person should come first and a buddy is the one you live and drink with, and he was not drinking with the men in charge.

chapters.title: More Than Just a Park
chapters.summary: Now living in Palm Coast, Conway names his passion the little links park and its Galimore Center pool, closed for over two years, which he has petitioned the city commissioners to restore as the one sanctuary children had from the hatred of the era, and argues that a people kept out of the political spectrum for decades cannot be expected to flood into civic life the moment a bill passes.

chapters.title: If He Goes, I Go
chapters.summary: At Dr. Hayling's suggestion Conway pays tribute to his mother Julia Conway, whose passion was for her sons to excel, and his father George Conway, who delivered groceries on Anastasia Island and was so loved across the white community that a judge stopped a bank manager from giving the family a hard time over a court order, and whose white butcher saved his job by telling the owner that if his father went for marching, he would go too.

chapters.title: They Fired Her Before Ten Years
chapters.summary: Conway describes his mother, a smart and loving woman who got a decent job at Flagler Hospital training nurses and giving newborns home remedies yet was fired before her tenth year to deny her a pension, and explains that the scarcity of pensions in Florida is what drew him north to land decent jobs and a pension of his own.

chapters.title: Praise for the ACCORD
chapters.summary: In closing the interview turns to Dr. Hayling's continued activism and to Conway's praise for the ACCORD organization, which Gwendolyn Duncan and her family took on years ago, and for Barbara Vickers, who worked to build a monument to the foot soldiers of the civil rights movement in St. Augustine.

loc_video.caption: Purcell Maurice Conway oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Saint Augustine, Florida, 2011 September 13
